title: How to Distinguish Between Your Intuition and Your Anxiety
meta_description: Learn the key differences between your intuition and anxiety. Stop confusing gut feelings with fear. Clear, practical guidance for sensitive people.
og_title: How to Distinguish Between Your Intuition and Your Anxiety
og_description: Learn the key differences between your intuition and anxiety. Stop confusing gut feelings with fear. Clear, practical guidance for sensitive people.
body:
You're Not Broken - You're Picking Up Everything

Let me guess. You've been called "too sensitive" more times than you can count. You feel other people's moods like they're your own. You walk into a room and instantly know something's off - even if everyone's smiling. And when you try to explain it, people look at you like you've grown a second head.

Here's the thing nobody told you: nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most people's. You're picking up signals - emotional, energetic, even psychic - that others literally can't feel. That's not a defect. It's a gift. But it comes with a catch.

The catch is this: when you feel everything so intensely, how do you know which signal is your intuition and which one is your anxiety? They feel the same in your body. They both show up as a knot in your stomach, a tightness in your chest, a voice that says "something's wrong." And if you've been told your whole life that your feelings are wrong, you've learned to distrust every single one of them. Right?!

So let's get clear on this. Because you can't work through the world as a sensitive person without learning to tell the difference. And I promise you - it's not as complicated as it seems.

The Core Difference: Fear vs. Knowing

Anxiety and intuition might feel similar in your body, but they come from completely different places. Anxiety is fear-based. It's your brain trying to protect you from threats - real or imagined. Intuition is knowing-based. It's your deeper self giving you information without the emotional charge.

Here's the simplest way to tell them apart: anxiety keeps you stuck. Intuition moves you forward.

Think about it. When anxiety hits, what does it do? It spins. It loops. It says "what if" over and over. It keeps you up at night replaying conversations and imagining worst-case scenarios. It doesn't solve anything - it just keeps you frozen in fear.

Intuition, on the other hand, feels clear. It doesn't need to repeat itself. It shows up once, with a sense of certainty, and then it's done. You don't have to convince yourself it's true - you just know. Does that land?

"Anxiety is the voice of fear trying to protect you from a world it doesn't trust. Intuition is the voice of your soul trying to guide you through a world it knows intimately. One tightens. The other opens."

Now, I know what you're thinking. "But Kalesh, my anxiety feels like it knows things too. It's told me to avoid people who turned out to be bad for me. So how is that different from intuition?"

Great question. Let me break it down.

The Body Doesn't Lie - But Your Brain Will

Your body is your most honest instrument. It doesn't lie, it doesn't manipulate, and it doesn't care about being polite. When you're anxious, your body goes into fight-or-flight. Your shoulders tense up toward your ears. Your breathing gets shallow. Your stomach clenches like you're about to be attacked. It's a stress response - pure and simple.

When you're receiving intuitive information, your body feels different. It might still feel intense, but it's not tight. It's more like a knowing that settles in your gut or your chest. Some people describe it as a feeling of expansion, or a quiet hum. It's not panicked. It's just... there.

Try this right now. Think of something that makes you genuinely anxious - a bill you can't pay, a conversation you're dreading, a decision you've been avoiding. Notice how your body responds. Chances are, you feel that familiar tightness somewhere. Your jaw might clench. Your shoulders might rise. That's your anxiety signature.

Now think of a time you just knew something. Maybe you knew a friend was going to call before they did. Maybe you knew not to take a certain job even though it looked perfect on paper. What did that feel like in your body? For most people, it's a softer sensation. A drop in the stomach, a warmth in the chest, a sudden clarity that doesn't need words.

Learning to tell the difference starts with noticing these physical cues. And the best way to do that is to practice - every single day.

Your Intuition Speaks Once - Anxiety Talks in Circles

Here's another way to tell them apart: intuition is efficient. It shows up, delivers the information, and gets out of the way. You might get a flash of insight, a sudden knowing, or a gut feeling that says "yes" or "no." And then it's gone. You don't have to sit there analyzing it because it already feels complete.

Anxiety, on the other hand, is a chatterbox. It doesn't know when to shut up. It'll start with one worry, then branch into five more, then loop back to the first one, then add a new layer of fear you hadn't even considered. It's like having a friend who keeps calling you with the same problem but never takes your advice. Exhausting, right?

If you're lying in bed at 2 AM going back and forth about a decision, that's not intuition. Intuition doesn't keep you up at night. It gives you clarity and then lets you sleep. Anxiety is the one that hands you a cup of coffee at midnight and says "let's go over this one more time."

So here's a practical tip: when you're trying to make a decision, notice how many times the thought comes back. If it's a one-time hit that feels clear, trust it. If it's a repeating loop that keeps getting louder, that's anxiety. Don't make decisions from the loop. Wait until the loop quiets down, and then check in with your gut again.

Anxiety Needs a Story - Intuition Just Knows

Anxiety loves a good narrative. It will create entire scenarios in your head - complete with dialogue, facial expressions, and outcomes - to justify why you should be afraid. "If you go to that party, everyone will judge you. You'll say something stupid. They'll talk about you afterward. You'll feel humiliated." Sound familiar?

Intuition doesn't do that. It doesn't need a story. It just gives you the bottom line. "Don't go." Or "Go." Or "Wait." No explanation required. You might not even know why you feel that way, but you know you feel it.

I had a client once who was trying to decide whether to move across the country for a job. She had a spreadsheet with pros and cons. She'd talked to everyone she knew. She'd made lists, researched neighborhoods, and calculated costs. She was completely stuck. When I asked her what her gut was saying, she said "I don't know - I can't hear it over all the noise."

That's the problem. Anxiety drowns out intuition with stories. The only way to hear your intuition is to stop telling yourself stories. Get quiet. Breathe. Ask yourself one question: "If I had to decide right now, with no thinking, what would I choose?" The first answer that comes - before your brain jumps in - that's your intuition.

Practical Steps to Tell Them Apart

Okay, let's get practical. Here are five things you can do right now to start distinguishing between your intuition and your anxiety.

1. Check Your Breath

When you feel that knot in your stomach, stop and notice your breathing. Is it shallow and fast? That's anxiety. Is it slow and steady? That's more likely intuition. Your breath is a direct line to your nervous system. If you can't tell what you're feeling, just breathe deeply for 30 seconds and see if the feeling changes. Anxiety usually intensifies when you breathe into it. Intuition stays the same or softens.

2. Ask "What Would Love Do?"

This sounds cheesy, I know. But it works. Anxiety is driven by fear, and fear is the opposite of love. So when you're trying to decide, ask yourself: "If I were acting from love right now, what would I do?" The answer is usually your intuition. If the answer feels heavy, tight, or punishing, it's probably anxiety dressed up as wisdom.

3. Give It 24 Hours

Intuition doesn't have a deadline. If you feel pressured to decide right now, that pressure is almost certainly anxiety. Give yourself permission to wait. Sleep on it. Take a walk. Take a shower. Let the feeling settle. If it's still there tomorrow with the same clarity, it's intuition. If it changes or fades, it was anxiety.

4. Journal Without Thinking

Get a notebook and write down whatever comes to mind about the decision. Don't edit. Don't judge. Just let it flow. After a few minutes, read what you wrote. Anxiety tends to show up as repetitive loops, questions without answers, and lots of "what ifs." Intuition shows up as short, direct statements. "I don't want to go." "This feels wrong." "I know I can trust them." Look for the sentences that feel true without needing proof.

5. Use Your Body as a Yes/No Machine

This is one of my favorite tools. Stand up and say the decision out loud. For example, "I'm going to take this job." Notice how your body responds. Does it feel like it's leaning forward, opening up, or relaxing? That's a yes. Does it feel like it's pulling back, tightening, or closing down? That's a no. Do the same with the opposite choice. "I'm not going to take this job." See which one feels more aligned. Your body knows the answer before your brain does.

If you want to go deeper with this practice, I highly recommend The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's a deep look at how trauma lives in the body and how to reconnect with your physical self. See on Amazon (paid link). This book changed how I work with clients - it's that good.

Why You've Been Confusing Them (And It's Not Your Fault)

Let me be really honest with you. You've been confusing intuition with anxiety because you were trained to. From the time you were a kid, you were told that your feelings were wrong. "You're too sensitive." "Stop overreacting." "You're imagining things." So you learned to doubt yourself. You learned to second-guess every feeling that came through.

And here's the kicker: when you doubt your intuition, it gets quieter. It stops speaking up because it knows you won't listen. But anxiety? Anxiety gets louder. It fills the void. It says "fine, I'll take over since you're not trusting yourself."

So now you have a situation where your intuition is whispering and your anxiety is screaming. No wonder you can't tell them apart. But here's the good news: you can retrain yourself. You can learn to hear the whisper again. It just takes practice and patience.

Another resource that's been incredibly helpful for this work is The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown. It's about letting go of who you think you're supposed to be and embracing who you actually are. See on Amazon (paid link). For sensitive people, this book is a lifeline.

The Bright Wound - Why You Feel Everything

I call this work "The Bright Wound" because your sensitivity is both your greatest gift and your biggest challenge. It's bright because it allows you to see, feel, and know things that others can't. It's a wound because the world isn't built for people like you. You've been hurt by your sensitivity - told it was wrong, punished for it, made to feel crazy.

But here's the truth: your sensitivity is not a flaw. It's a superpower. The fact that you feel things deeply means you can connect with people on a level that most can't. It means you can read a room, sense danger, and know when someone needs help. It means you have access to information that's invisible to others.

The only problem is that you've been using the same radar to pick up your own fears. And when fear and knowing get mixed together, it's chaos. But now you know how to separate them. You have tools. You have awareness. And you have permission to trust yourself.

For those of you who want to dive even deeper into understanding your intuitive gifts, I recommend The Highly Sensitive Person by Elaine Aron. See on Amazon (paid link). It's the book that made me feel sane for the first time in my life. It explains the science behind what you're experiencing and gives practical strategies for navigating the world as a sensitive person.

Trust Is a Muscle - You Have to Build It

Here's the final thing I want you to know: learning to trust your intuition is not a one-time decision. It's a practice. You're going to get it wrong sometimes. You're going to confuse anxiety with intuition and make a choice that doesn't work out. That's okay. That's how you learn.

Every time you trust your intuition and it's right, you build a little more trust. Every time you realize you were listening to anxiety, you get a little better at telling them apart. It's a muscle. You have to work it.

Start small. Practice with low-stakes decisions. What to eat for dinner. Whether to go for a walk. Which route to take to work. Notice what your body says. Notice what your brain says. See if you can tell the difference. Over time, you'll get so good at it that you'll be able to trust your gut on the big stuff too.

And when you do, your whole life will change. You'll stop second-guessing yourself. You'll stop asking everyone else for their opinion. You'll start making decisions from a place of clarity and confidence. You'll sleep better. You'll stress less. You'll feel more like yourself than you ever have.

That's the gift of learning to distinguish between your intuition and your anxiety. It's not just about making better decisions - it's about reclaiming your trust in yourself. And that's the most powerful thing you can do.

If you're ready to take this work deeper and start trusting yourself on a whole new level, I've put together a free quiz that helps you identify your intuitive style and where you're getting stuck. Click here to take the quiz and start your journey to trusting yourself again.
